I do see the problem you are describing but I think it depends on the point of view of the user. 

I personally think of the `level` as being the loglevel at which the log messages 
get displayed on the console, since the `StreamHandler` gets the same loglevel.
I do not think about the loglevel assigned to the logger object itself, because on its own
it does not do much. 
The changes proposed here shift the interpretation of the `level` parameter slightly.
Currently it is referring to the logger object and the attached `StreamHandler`. After applying
the changes it is only referring to the loglevel of the `StreamHandler`. 
Fixing the loglevel of the logger object to `DEBUG` might be unexpected in the sense that
a user might not expect the logger object to be set up this way, but not in the sense that this will
result in unexpected behavior.
Setting the logger objects loglevel to always be the same as the `StreamHandler`s loglevel (as it is currently done) does however result in unexpected behavior as described in #57 . 

The advantage of fixing the logger objects loglevel to  `DEBUG` is that the attached handlers
(presumably  a `StreamHandler` and a `FileHandler` in most cases) are able to have independent loglevels.

I have thought about this change for a bit, but I came to the conclusion that this change is
in line with the goal of this package to increase the ease of use of the `logging` module.
Even though this package is just a thin wrapper it feels a bit more “higher level” then the
`logging` module. One big part of this for me personally is not really having to think about the
logger object itself. It feels in a way more like interacting with the `StreamHandler`. 

A different approach would be to add a new parameter `streamLogLevel` with similar functionality to existing parameter `fileLogLevel`. This would in turn require more changes to
the code.

Aight, so here's my update:

1. I just added a clubbing switch to convert. 

2. The behaviour where a list with a single dict entry becomes a clubbed table is actually not my change but that of @lekic, which came in with 1.0.3. All I did was extend the same behaviour of embedded lists to top level JSON lists. I agree with @Lekic's idea however. Here's my reasoning: 

Consider the following json:

    [{"a":1}, {"a":2}]

Here we agree it should be clubbed, right? So we get a table. Now take away one of the dictionaries from the list. Wait, what happened, why does my output now look completely different? First I had a table, now I have a list with one entry, itself being a table? 

Would you agree that for consistency reasons, if clubbing is activated, a list of one elementsshould behave the same as a list of two elements, iff they have the same properties (thus can be clubbed)? Otherwise I'd find it a surprising result. 

So now, if people don't want this they can just convert with the `clubbing = False` option. In that case, again, it behaves the same if the list has one or multiple elements.

Regarding your dict suggestion: a double-splat? I don't know, I mean, I'll use that code if you want me to, but it's up to you. Not the way I'd write it (obviously)... this is Python. It's supposed to be easy and make sense. We could use C if we want the code to run faster, be less easy to write, and have questionable sensibility.

There's no way that using `.format()` makes a worthwhile speed difference over `.replace()` in a string of this size... This is 2016. I'm assuming that `.format()` is actually faster than multiple `.replace()` calls, although I'm not familiar with it's internals. But if this optimization is a dealbreaker for someone's machine's capabilities/performance, then I think they've got bigger problems, like running building & running Google Play Music Desktop Player...

Please just tell me what you want (with a succinct explanation of _why_, but only if you're feeling generous); neither of our solutions seems _particularly_ bad, and I don't have time to debate every line of code. I think we'd both be happier and have more time to do more worthwhile things with our lives if we didn't. If you want me to include some code that I think is completely stupid and nonsensical (this seems like an unlikely scenario), I'll let you know, and/or just not make the change/commit myself out of principal.

Mostly (a) I don't like global variables in general, and (b) I don't really understand your use case of wanting to change the decimal value for lots of unit tests all at once.  Why did you find it useful to be able to do that?

Personally, I find it hard when reading a unit test to understand how precise each test really is when these variables are at module scope.  I can't see the definitions of these variables when I am looking at a test function, so these variables just obfuscate my understanding of how precise the test is.  The hard coded numbers in contrast are self-documenting.  

For instance, some of the times where you used `decimal_precise`, you had a comment saying that it was actually precise to machine precision.  In those cases, I switched to using decimal=12 and removed the comment.  That seems much clearer to me.  

In general, I feel like you should know how accurate a unit test is when you write it.  I generally start with decimal=5 or 6, and only drop it if I understand why the test should not be expected to pass at that level of precision.  Very often there is a good reason to only use decimal=2 or 3, but it is certainly test-specific, so I don't really see the point of having the decimal value for lots of tests set at module scope.  I'd rather have non-default decimal values be set for each test individually to the value appropriate for that specific test.

The use case is when the output pex is not in cwd, i.e. `-o dir/output.pex`. In that case, we should be supporting the case where the .pexrc is in `dir` as per a precedent set in the tests, however I see this being practical beyond testing situations. 

Previously, the check was for os.path.dirname(sys.argv[0]) to find this .pexrc in the same dir as the output pex at runtime, which works fine because sys.argv[0] is the pex itself, however, at build time, sys.argv[0] is the pex cli binary, which can be different than the dir of the output pex i.e. `pex requests -o dir/output.pex`. In this case, where I have .pexrc in `dir`, special accommodations are needed to make sure that the pex build routine can find that pexrc at build time. 

Like you say, this precedent is set largely by the tests, however having a pexrc in the same location as the pex itself seems like a reasonable case to cover to me.

It doesn't need to be baked into deisctl! Since it's a glorified fleetctl wrapper we can use our own tooling for this!

For example, using the [ping example service from fleet](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/coreos/fleet/master/examples/ping.service):

```
><> cd $DEISCTL_UNITS
><> wget [ping.service]
><> mv ping.service deis-ping.service
><> deisctl install ping
deis-ping.service: loaded                                 
><> deisctl start ping
deis-ping.service: active/running                                 
><> deisctl journal ping
-- Logs begin at Wed 2015-07-01 09:14:37 . --

Jul 01 16:49:27 deis-03 systemd[1]: Starting PING...

Jul 01 16:49:31 deis-03 bash[1675]: ping
Jul 01 16:49:32 deis-03 bash[1675]: ping
Jul 01 16:49:33 deis-03 bash[1675]: ping
```

Another solution would be to change the last few calls to fleetctl to the following:

```
$ DEISCTL_UNITS=/tmp deisctl install store-volume
$ DEISCTL_UNITS=/tmp deisctl start store-volume
$ rm /tmp/deis-store-volume.service
```

I agree with @Blystad that using existing tooling would make for a happier installation experience, but the controller fix would also be a better solution so we don't have to hack this in. This is an officially supported workflow so we should make this as smooth as possible for users, in my opinion. :)

Just to restate, saving benchmark data somewhere and comparing against previous runs would be unreliable due to virtualization and contention in TravisCI. We can talk about instruction counters I guess (since those shouldn't care about preemption right?), what would be useful?

And testing on pypy further complicates things because of warmup (the plugin has such feature but you need to set it correctly).

To transform bytes into unicode instance use `vim_u` function. Apparently bindings still have nothing like this.

```Patch
diff --git a/powerline/bindings/vim/__init__.py b/powerline/bindings/vim/__init__.py
index e617943..398aa40 100644
--- a/powerline/bindings/vim/__init__.py
+++ b/powerline/bindings/vim/__init__.py
@@ -61,6 +61,12 @@ python_to_vim_types = {
 python_to_vim_types[float] = python_to_vim_types[int]
 
 
+def vim_u(s):
+	if isinstance(s, unicode):
+		return s
+	return s.decode(vim_encoding, 'powerline_vim_strtrans_error')
+
+
 def python_to_vim(o):
 	return python_to_vim_types[type(o)](o)
 
```
